edges.quote: In the Bite, the lords of the Three Sisters had taken advantage of the chaos of Aegon’s Conquest to declare themselves a free nation and crown Lady Marla of House Sunderland their queen.
edges.note: The disorder of the Conquest is the precondition the Sistermen exploit to declare independence; it does not force their revolt.
edges.rationale: ENABLES over CAUSES: the Three Sisters lords make a free decision to rebel using the Conquest's chaos as opportunity, not as a forcing cause (agency-collapse check).

edges.quote: House Hoare had ruled the ironmen for long centuries, only to be extinguished in a single night when Aegon unleashed Balerion’s fires on Harrenhal. Though Harren the Black and his sons perished in those flames, Qhorin Volmark of Harlaw, whose grandmother had been a younger sister of Harren’s grandsire, declared himself the rightful heir “of the black line,” and
edges.note: Harren's death in the burning of Harrenhal opens the succession Qhorin Volmark claims.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES (event-target form) rather than CAUSES: Volmark's claim of the black line is his own decision seizing on the vacancy left by Harren's death, a human choice mediating the link (agency-collapse check).

edges.quote: Aegon Targaryen put an end to the fighting. He descended on the islands in 2 AC, riding Balerion.
edges.note: Volmark's rival kingship (and the fratricidal war it touched off among ironborn claimants) is what draws Aegon's invasion in 2 AC.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES not CAUSES/TRIGGERS: Aegon's decision to personally intervene is a free royal decision responding to the ironborn succession crisis, not a mechanical consequence of it.

edges.quote: With the submission of the Three Sisters and the Iron Islands, all of Westeros south of the Wall was now ruled by Aegon Targaryen, save Dorne alone. So it was to Dorne that the Dragon next turned his attention.
edges.note: Pacifying the Iron Islands frees Aegon's attention and forces to finally turn on Dorne.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: the Iron Islands' submission is a precondition (Aegon now has nothing else to subdue) but Aegon still freely chooses to invade Dorne rather than being forced to.

edges.quote: With the submission of the Three Sisters and the Iron Islands, all of Westeros south of the Wall was now ruled by Aegon Targaryen, save Dorne alone. So it was to Dorne that the Dragon next turned his attention.
edges.note: The text names both the Three Sisters' and the Iron Islands' submission as the joint precondition for Aegon turning to Dorne.
edges.rationale: ENABLES for the same reason as A-E04; the Sistermen's submission is the other named half of the precondition, not a forcing cause on its own.

edges.quote: Riding Meraxes, the queen descended out of a clear blue sky and set the Planky Town ablaze, the fires leaping from boat to boat until the whole mouth of the Greenblood was choked with burning flotsam, and the pillar of smoke could be seen as far away as Sunspear.
edges.note: Rhaenys's burning of the Planky Town is explicitly the opening act ("first blood") of the First Dornish War.
edges.rationale: Structural PART_OF: it is the war's opening battle, not a separate causal claim; matches the existing PART_OF pattern already on Defenestration of Sunspear.

edges.quote: When word of the Defenestration of Sunspear reached the Hellholt, Lord Tyrell gathered his remaining strength and set off across the sands. His announced intention was to capture Vaith, march east along the river, retake Sunspear and the shadow city, and punish Lord Rosby’s murderers. But somewhere east of the Hellholt amidst the red sands, Tyrell and his entire army disappeared.
edges.note: News of Rosby's murder at Sunspear is the explicit reason Tyrell marches out from the Hellholt — the march on which he vanishes.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES (event-target form): Tyrell's march to avenge Rosby is his own decision; the defenestration does not mechanically force his disappearance, it drives the choice that leads to it.

edges.quote: In 7 AC, Orys Baratheon and the other lords who had been taken captive on the Boneway were ransomed back to King's Landing for their weight in gold, but on their return it was found that the Widow-lover had lopped off each man’s sword hand, so they might never again take up swords against Dorne. In retaliation, King Aegon himself descended on the mountain fastnesses of the Wyls with Balerion, and reduced half a dozen of their keeps and watchtowers to heaps of molten stone.
edges.note: Seed row 23: the Widow-lover's maiming of the ransomed captives draws Aegon's direct Balerion reprisal on the Wyl mountain keeps.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES over TRIGGERS: the text's own ‘In retaliation’ reads as immediate, but Aegon's decision to personally fly to war is still a human choice mediating the link (agency-collapse check), so it is routed as a decision-event target rather than collapsed into CAUSES/TRIGGERS.

edges.quote: In 7 AC, Orys Baratheon and the other lords who had been taken captive on the Boneway were ransomed back to King's Landing for their weight in gold, but on their return it was found that the Widow-lover had lopped off each man’s sword hand, so they might never again take up swords against Dorne.
edges.note: Structural containment for the new mint.
edges.rationale: PART_OF: a marquee sub-beat of the named war, not an umbrella parent.

edges.quote: In retaliation, King Aegon himself descended on the mountain fastnesses of the Wyls with Balerion, and reduced half a dozen of their keeps and watchtowers to heaps of molten stone.
edges.note: Structural containment for the new mint.
edges.rationale: PART_OF: a marquee sub-beat of the named war, not an umbrella parent.

edges.quote: Lord Orys was taken captive during the Dornish War and suffered the loss of his sword hand. When ransomed back, his lordship asked the king to be relieved of his duties. “The King’s Hand should have a hand,” he said.
edges.note: The maiming is the explicit, stated reason Orys resigns as Hand of the King.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES (disposition/character-target form): his own mutilation drives Orys's personal decision to resign, not a mechanical consequence.

edges.quote: But as Meraxes banked above the Hellholt, a defender atop the castle's highest tower triggered a scorpion, and a yard-long iron bolt caught the queen's dragon in the right eye. Meraxes did not die at once, but came crashing to earth in mortal agony
edges.note: Seed row 24: the scorpion bolt fells Meraxes, and the dragon's crash is the immediate mechanism of Rhaenys's death. [S204 adjudication: TRIGGERS->CAUSES tier-1 — death-from-downing undisputed; MECHANISM disputed (fall vs later torture), noted not tiered]
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: no decision intervenes between dragon and rider — Meraxes's fatal fall is the direct physical mechanism of Rhaenys's death (whichever of the disputed accounts of her exact end is true), the tightest link on the ladder.

edges.quote: But as Meraxes banked above the Hellholt, a defender atop the castle's highest tower triggered a scorpion, and a yard-long iron bolt caught the queen's dragon in the right eye.
edges.note: Structural containment for the new mint.
edges.rationale: PART_OF: the war's single greatest Targaryen loss, not an umbrella parent.

edges.quote: Rhaenys Targaryen, sister and wife to King Aegon |, perished at the Hellholt in Dorne in the 10th year After the Conquest.
edges.note: Structural containment for the new mint.
edges.rationale: PART_OF: a marquee sub-beat of the named war, not an umbrella parent.

edges.quote: The next two years were the years of the Dragon’s Wroth. Every castle in Dorne, save Sunspear, was burned thrice over, as Balerion and Vhagar returned time and time again.
edges.note: Seed row 24: Rhaenys's death is what the two-year reprisal campaign is named and dated from ("the next two years" after her 10 AC death).
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: Aegon and Visenya's grief-driven decision to burn Dorne repeatedly is a human/royal choice responding to the death, not a mechanical CAUSES; the campaign is itself a sustained decision-event.

edges.quote: The next two years were the years of the Dragon’s Wroth. Every castle in Dorne, save Sunspear, was burned thrice over, as Balerion and Vhagar returned time and time again.
edges.note: Structural containment for the new mint.
edges.rationale: PART_OF: the war's climactic reprisal phase, explicitly named within the war's own narrative, not an umbrella parent.

edges.quote: Aegon was the Lord of the Seven Kingdoms; therefore, he should have seven champions, Queen Visenya decided. Thus did the Kingsguard come into being;
edges.note: The Dornish counter-assassination attempts on Aegon and Visenya during the Dragon's Wroth are what push Visenya to institute the seven-strong Kingsguard (governance section, lines 279-283, same unit). [S204 adjudication: re-quoted to the founding-decision sentence per fresh-verify]
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: Visenya's decision to found the Kingsguard is her own free choice provoked by the repeated attacks, not a forced consequence of them.

edges.quote: The most infamous act of that bloody age occurred in 12 AC, when Wyl of Wyl, the Widow-lover, arrived uninvited at the wedding of Ser Jon Cafferen, heir to Fawnton, to Alys Oakheart, daughter to the Lord of Old Oak.
edges.note: The text explicitly frames the wedding massacre as the single most infamous act of the Dragon's Wroth period ("that bloody age").
edges.rationale: SUB_BEAT_OF: the wedding raid is a discrete atrocity inside the named reprisal-era hub, giving the previously causally-dark roster node its first structural edge.

edges.quote: By then Dorne was a smoking desert, beset by famine, plague, and blight. “A blasted land,” traders from the Free Cities called it.
edges.note: The devastation of the Dragon's Wroth is what leaves Dorne, per Grand Maester Clegg later in the same passage, without the strength to keep fighting, making Nymor's peace overture possible. [S204 adjudication: tier-2 — causal link rests on Grand Maester Clegg's attributed analysis ('Dorne no longer had the strength to fight')]
edges.rationale: ENABLES: the ruin of Dorne is a precondition for peace, not a forcing cause — Nymor still freely chooses to sue for peace rather than fight on (House Martell ‘still remained Unbowed, Unbent, Unbroken’ in the same line).

edges.quote: King Aegon read Prince Nymor's words in open court, stone-faced and silent, whilst seated on the Iron Throne. When he rose afterward, men said, his hand was dripping blood. He burned the letter and never spoke of it again, but that night he mounted Balerion and flew off across the waters of Blackwater Bay, to Dragonstone upon its smoking mountain.
edges.note: Section note: the mysterious letter is the proximate trigger that makes Aegon, on the point of refusing peace, relent overnight and agree to Nymor's terms the next morning.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: Aegon's overnight change of heart and subsequent assent to peace is his own sovereign decision provoked by the letter's unknown contents, not a mechanical CAUSES — the text is explicit that the king was 'on the point of refusing' until the letter intervened.

new_nodes.name: Maiming of Orys Baratheon and the ransomed lords
new_nodes.identity: In 7 AC, Orys Baratheon and the other lords ransomed back from Dornish captivity return to King's Landing with their sword hands lopped off by the Widow-lover, Wyl of Wyl, so they can never again bear arms against Dorne.

new_nodes.name: Aegon's reprisal on the Wyls
new_nodes.identity: In retaliation for the maiming of the ransomed captives, King Aegon personally descends on the mountain fastnesses of House Wyl with Balerion, reducing half a dozen of their keeps and watchtowers to molten stone; the Wyls survive by hiding in caves and tunnels.

new_nodes.name: Death of Meraxes
new_nodes.identity: During Aegon's renewed assault on the Hellholt, a Dornish defender's scorpion bolt catches the dragon Meraxes in the right eye; the dragon does not die at once but crashes to earth in mortal agony, destroying the castle's highest tower and part of its curtain wall.

new_nodes.name: Death of Rhaenys Targaryen
new_nodes.identity: Queen Rhaenys Targaryen perishes at the Hellholt in 10 AC when her dragon Meraxes is shot down; whether she lost her seat and fell, was crushed beneath the dragon, or survived only to be tortured to death by the Ullers is disputed by the sources, and her body was never recovered.

new_nodes.name: The Dragon's Wroth
new_nodes.identity: The two years following Rhaenys Targaryen's death, in which Aegon and Visenya burn every Dornish stronghold save Sunspear thrice over and place bounties on Dornish lords' heads, while the Dornish answer with assassinations and, in 12 AC, the Cafferen-Oakheart wedding massacre.

new_nodes.name: Nymor's letter to Aegon
new_nodes.identity: Princess Deria delivers a sealed letter from her father, Prince Nymor of Dorne, to King Aegon on the Iron Throne in open court; the letter's contents were never revealed, but reading it visibly shakes the king and leads him, after a night alone on Dragonstone, to accept Nymor's peace terms.

flags: creation-of-the-office-of-grand-maester left causally dark: the unit narrates it as Aegon's own initiative (line 243, 'feeling that the realm might benefit from such wisdom') with no upstream trigger or downstream causal consequence stated in this section; only role edges (AGENT_IN) apply.

volmark-conspiracy MOTIVATES invasion-of-the-iron-islands (A-E03) is grounded on Volmark's claim as one of several destabilizing factors (Lodos's rebellion and other claimants are narrated in the same paragraph but have no roster node in this section) — flagging in case a fuller Iron Islands chaos node is minted elsewhere and this edge should be re-pointed.

rhaenyss-parley-with-meria-martell.node.md exists in graph/nodes/events/ (pre-war 1 AC parley, 'return at your peril') but is not in this section's roster and was left unwired to stay in scope; it is a plausible upstream ENABLES/precursor to first-dornish-war for a future pass.
the-dragons-wroth ac_year set to 11 (approximate start) since the unit only states 'the next two years' after Rhaenys's 10 AC death; no exact year is given in this text.

harvest.note: Septons speculated Nymor's letter was ensorceled, written using a vial of Queen Rhaenys's own blood for ink — blood magic motif worth tracking.
Queen Rhaenys's 'rule of six' (and 'rule of thumb') ruling on lawful wife-beating, delivered at the Aegonfort — notable in-universe legal custom, not yet a node.
Visenya's fool Lord Monkeyface (a hirsute hunchback) choked to death on a peach pit; she then dressed an ape in his clothes and called it cleverer — vivid character color, unattached to any node.
Description of Aegon's royal progresses: guesting with great lords, 'feasts and balls and hunts and hawking,' six traveling maesters — hospitality-pattern material for a later pass.
Origin of Aegon's High Hill, Visenya's Hill, and the Hill of Rhaenys as the three hills of King's Landing take their names from the three Targaryen siblings.

dropped_at_adjudication.reason: two-hop compression (Garmon death -> vengeance -> campaign -> Meraxes); quote grounds neither hop cleanly
